metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm whether a nit/recall eval for `code-review-base` already exists (and locate it), or add a `code-review-base.eval.yaml` eval spec that gates the nit-suppression and recall properties cited in the AC.
findings.finding: The AC 'nit/recall eval passes' asserts that a nit-suppression / recall eval for code-review-base exists and will pass, but no such eval spec (e.g. code-review-base.eval.yaml) exists anywhere in src/rebar/llm/eval_specs/ — the only code-review-adjacent spec is code-quality.eval.yaml for the separate `code-quality` reviewer.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — '[ ] Base prompt has exactly one positive-framed deference line; nit/recall eval passes.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add the concrete proving command (e.g., `rebar eval nit-recall` or equivalent script path and invocation) to AC1 and AC4 so an executor can run the eval in-session and produce a pass/fail artifact.
findings.finding: The ACs 'nit/recall eval passes' and 'plan-review evals green' name desired outcomes but provide no concrete proving command (no eval script name, no CLI invocation, no test path), leaving an executor unable to confirm these criteria without additional discovery.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC1 ('nit/recall eval passes') and AC4 ('plan-review evals green')
findings.suggested_fix: In AC1, replace 'nit/recall eval passes' with 'nit/recall eval passes (`scripts/run_eval.sh nit-recall` or equivalent, producing a ≥threshold score)'; in AC4, replace 'plan-review evals green' with 'plan-review eval suite passes (`scripts/run_eval.sh plan-review`, all cases green)' — substituting the actual script paths used in the repo.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a pointer in AC1 (or the Scope section) to the eval harness that constitutes the 'nit/recall eval' — file path, command, and pass threshold — so the criterion is self-contained.
findings.finding: AC1 references a 'nit/recall eval' as its verification mechanism, but the plan body never describes what that eval is, where it lives, or how to run it — leaving the criterion unverifiable without external knowledge.
findings.suggested_fix: Append to AC1: 'Verified by running `<eval-command>` against the eval corpus at `<path>`; substantive-finding recall ≥ 2.3 and nit count ≤ baseline.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an eval criterion (or name an existing code-review verifier eval) in AC2/AC3 that confirms the contradiction rubric, caller verification, arithmetic gate, and absence-search guidance are active in code-review-verify.md's output — analogous to the 'nit/recall eval' named for the base-prompt change.
findings.finding: The verifier additions in AC2/AC3 (contradiction rubric, caller verification, arithmetic gate, absence search, AST/LSP preference) introduce substantive new behavioral guidance to code-review-verify.md, but no corresponding eval or observable verification criterion is specified to confirm these additions actually produce the intended verifier behavior.
findings.suggested_fix: Extend AC2 with a verification clause such as: 'code-review verifier eval shows evidence-contradiction and caller-verification checks firing on a curated diff set that includes authoritative-tone-only claims and unreachable-caller assertions.' This mirrors AC1's eval reference and makes the verifier additions falsifiable.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Update `VERIFIER_RULES` / `VERIFIER_RULES_SCAFFOLD` in `src/rebar/llm/review_kernel/verify.py` to include the new domain-agnostic discipline rules (contradiction rubric, caller verification, arithmetic gate, absence-search), or document why these rules are intentionally not part of the shared scaffold.
findings.finding: The plan adds new behavioral verifier rules to code-review-verify.md (contradiction rubric, caller verification, arithmetic gate, absence-search), but verify.py defines VERIFIER_RULES as 'the single discoverable source' of verifier soft rules 'recorded ONCE here' — the canonical scaffold will become stale/incomplete relative to what the prompt actually instructs, splitting the authoritative source.
findings.location: src/rebar/llm/review_kernel/verify.py:64-84 (VERIFIER_RULES / VERIFIER_RULES_SCAFFOLD) vs What section of the plan
findings.suggested_fix: Either add the new rules as additional entries in the VERIFIER_RULES tuple in verify.py (keeping them domain-agnostic), or add a code comment to verify.py noting that code-review-specific extensions live in code-review-verify.md and explaining why they are intentionally out-of-scaffold.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Note in the plan (or implementation notes) that both `code-review-base.md` and `code-review-verify.md` must pass the `write_front_matter` round-trip invariant enforced by `test_all_code_review_prompts_are_canonical_front_matter_fixed_points`; front-matter must not be manually reformatted.
findings.finding: The plan names edits to both `code-review-base.md` and `code-review-verify.md` but does not enumerate `test_all_code_review_prompts_are_canonical_front_matter_fixed_points` (in `tests/unit/test_code_review_ws2.py`, line 145) as a constraint: this test runs `write_front_matter` round-trip over every `code-review-*.md` and will fail if either edited file's front-matter is not in canonical form after the edit. This is a real gating test the implementer must satisfy.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to Scope or AC: 'Front-matter of both edited files must remain canonical (write_front_matter round-trip = identity); run `write_front_matter(*_split_front_matter_raw(text))` to normalize after any front-matter touch.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Cite the path or identifier of the in-repo experiment artifact (eval script, results file, or eval run ID) so the 'recall 2.0→2.33' claim and the 'nit/recall eval passes' AC are independently reproducible.
findings.finding: The plan's primary justification for adding the positive-framed deference line to code-review-base.md is an 'in-repo experiment (recall 2.0->2.33 substantive with fewer nits, Sonnet tier),' but no experiment artifact, file, or path is cited, and a codebase search finds nothing matching this experiment. The AC 'nit/recall eval passes' presupposes a reproducible eval, which cannot be validated without a locatable artifact.
findings.location: ## What — first bullet; ## Acceptance Criteria — AC 1
findings.suggested_fix: Add a `## Evidence` section (or inline reference) pointing to the eval file(s), e.g. `tests/evals/code_review_nit_recall/` or a Langfuse run ID, so the claimed metric can be re-run to confirm the AC.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify what 'EXISTING sub-questions' refers to in code-review-verify.md (the file has no such named list); either point to the specific location where the new rubrics attach, or acknowledge that new structure will be added (updating the 'no contract change' claim accordingly).
findings.finding: The What section claims the verifier additions 'feed the EXISTING sub-questions' of code-review-verify.md, but reading that file in full reveals it has no named sub-question list — only four verifier rules and a binary sub-answers note — so there are no existing sub-questions to feed into.
findings.location: What section — 'absence-claim active-search feeding the EXISTING sub-questions (no new axis, no contract change)'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a one-sentence positive rationale for the AST/LSP-over-grep preference in the plan (e.g., 'structural tools give cross-reference and type-level evidence that textual grep cannot, which matters for caller-verification and reachability sub-questions') so the implementer can scope and calibrate the guidance correctly.
findings.finding: The plan asserts a guidance preference for AST/LSP/code-graph tools over grep in the verifier but provides no positive rationale explaining why this preference fits the code-review verification context — leaving the implementer without a principled basis for when to apply or override it.
findings.location: What section / AC line: 'Verifier guidance prefers AST/LSP tools when available, falls back to grep.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm whether AST/LSP/code-graph tools are actually available (or planned for availability) in the verifier's agentic runtime, and either cite the provisioning plan or scope the guidance to grep-only until tooling is confirmed.
findings.finding: The plan states verifier guidance will 'prefer AST/LSP/code-graph tools over grep when available,' but the existing code-review-verify.md executes in the same file-tool-only agentic environment as the base reviewer, and no AST, LSP, or code-graph tools appear in the tool list or execution configuration. This is an unverified capability assertion: the preferred tooling branch may not exist in the runtime.
findings.location: ## What — second bullet; ## Acceptance Criteria — AC 3

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify in the plan (or the prompt diff) whether the absence-search guidance is co-located inline with the relevant binary sub-question descriptions (e.g. `no_existing_mitigation`, `path_reachable`) in code-review-verify.md, or justify why a standalone preamble placement suffices.
findings.finding: The plan adds 'absence-claim active-search feeding the EXISTING sub-questions' but the existing code-review-verify.md prompt contains no per-sub-question structure — it lists binary sub-answers as a flat block with no section anchors. The plan does not specify WHERE in the prompt the absence-search guidance is co-located with the sub-questions it disciplines (path_reachable, no_existing_mitigation), risking it being added as a disconnected general instruction that the LLM does not associate with the specific sub-questions.
findings.location: code-review-verify.md lines 36-38 (binary sub-answers block) / Plan 'What' section (AC2)

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
